coredata.dc:title: DNA-functionalized carbon quantum dots for electrochemical detection of pyocyanin: A quorum sensing molecule in Pseudomonas aeruginosa
coredata.dc:creator: Thulasinathan, Boobalan
D, Sujatha
Murugan, Sethupathi
Panda, Subhendu K.
Veerapandian, Murugan
Manickam, Pandiaraj
coredata.dc:description: The electrochemical biosensing strategy for pyocyanin (PYO), a virulent quorum-sensing molecule responsible for Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections, was developed by mimicking its extracellular DNA interaction. Calf thymus DNA (ct-DNA) functionalized amine-containing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used as a biomimetic receptor for electrochemical sensing of PYO as low as 37 nM in real urine sample. The ct-DNA-based biosensor enabled the selective measurement of PYO in the presence of other interfering species. Calibration and validation of the PYO sensor platform were demonstrated in buffer solution (0–100 μM), microbial culture media (0–100 μM), artificial urine (0–400 μM), and real urine sample (0–250 μM). The sensor capability was successfully implemented for point-of-care (POC) detection of PYO release from Pseudomonas aeruginosa strains during lag and stationary phases. Cross-reactivity of the sensing platform was also tested in other bacterial species such as Bacillus subtilis, Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Shigella dysenteriae, Staphylococcus aureus, and Streptococcus pneumoniae. Potential clinical implementation of the ct-DNA-based sensor was manifested in detecting the PYO in P. aeruginosa cultured baby diaper and sanitary napkin. Our results highlight that the newly developed ct-DNA-based sensing platform can be used as a potential candidate for real-time POC diagnosis of Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection in clinical samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ESKAPE Pathogens
Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Pyocyanin
ct-DNA
Carbon quantum dots
Biomimetic receptors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modification of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs
Measurement of PYO release from P. aeruginosa PAO1
Detection of PYO in vitro in baby diaper and sanitary napkin surface and validation
Electrochemical characterization of the ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs sensor assembly
Analytical performances of ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs modified electrode
Electrochemical PYO detection in P. aeruginosa – PAO1 (MTCC 3541) culture
Electrochemical interference of other bacterial cultures
Electrochemical signal in vivo in diaper and napkin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Comparison of UV–vis absorption spectra of NH2-CQDs and ct-DNA conjugated at the surface of NH2-CQDs. (Inset: luminescent properties of NH2-CQDs under normal daylight (i) and (ii) UV light (Excitation wavelength: 254 nm); (B) Fluorescence spectra of NH2 – CQDs at various excitation wavelengths confirming the optical properties of CQDs; (C) Comparison of FTIR spectra of CQDs, NH2-CQDs and ct-DNA conjugated at the surface of NH2-CQDs; (D) RAMAN spectra CQDs, NH2-CQDs and ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs.

High-resolution deconvoluted XPS spectra of N1s, O1s and P2p from ct-DNA functionalized NH2-CQDs (A–C) and pristine ct-DNA (D–F). Plausible mechanism proposed for the conjugation of ct-DNA with NH2-CQDs through electrostatic interaction (G).

CV measurementsof the bare (a), NH2-CQDs (b), and ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs (c) modified electrodes and EIS measurements of bare (a), ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs (b) and NH2-CQDs modified electrodes (c) (Inset: equivalent circuit used to model EIS data (top) and the enlarged version of the Rct curves). (C) Scans rate performed from 10 to 100 mV s−1 at the surface of ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs (D) linear plot of Log I p vs. Log ν of the scan rate. The measurements were performed in 5 mM K3 [Fe(CN)6]/K4 [Fe(CN)6].

SWV current peak of PBS (A) and artificial urine medium (B) with PYO linear range of 0–100 μM and 0–400 μM in PBS at pH 7, respectively. Calibration plot of PBS (C) and artificial urine medium (D). SWV scans were performed from −0.5 to 0 V at a frequency of 15 Hz and an amplitude voltage of 50 mV.

SWV current peak of LB culture medium with spiked PYO linear range of 0–100 μM at pH 7.4 and undiluted real urine sample with spiked PYO linear range of 0–250 μM (A–B); Calibration plot of LB culture medium and undiluted real urine sample (C–D).
Pictorial representation of electrode modification with ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs assembly and electrochemical measurement of PYO from artificial urine, culture medium, baby diaper and sanitary napkin.

head.ce:title: DNA-functionalized carbon quantum dots for electrochemical detection of pyocyanin: A quorum sensing molecule in Pseudomonas aeruginosa

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrodics & Electrocatalysis Division, CSIR - Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CECRI), Karaikudi, 630003, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electrodics & Electrocatalysis Division, CSIR - Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CECRI), Karaikudi-630003, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroplating and Metal Finishing Division, CSIR - Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CECRI), Karaikudi, 630003, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electroplating and Metal Finishing Division, CSIR - Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CECRI), Karaikudi-630003, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Electrodics and Electrocatalysis Division, CSIR-Central Electrochemical Research Institute Karaikudi, 630003, Tamil Nadu, India.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Academy of Scientific & Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad, Uttar Pradesh 201 002, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electrochemical biosensing strategy for pyocyanin (PYO), a virulent quorum-sensing molecule responsible for Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections, was developed by mimicking its extracellular DNA interaction. Calf thymus DNA (ct-DNA) functionalized amine-containing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used as a biomimetic receptor for electrochemical sensing of PYO as low as 37 nM in real urine sample. The ct-DNA-based biosensor enabled the selective measurement of PYO in the presence of other interfering species. Calibration and validation of the PYO sensor platform were demonstrated in buffer solution (0–100 μM), microbial culture media (0–100 μM), artificial urine (0–400 μM), and real urine sample (0–250 μM). The sensor capability was successfully implemented for point-of-care (POC) detection of PYO release from Pseudomonas aeruginosa strains during lag and stationary phases. Cross-reactivity of the sensing platform was also tested in other bacterial species such as Bacillus subtilis, Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Shigella dysenteriae, Staphylococcus aureus, and Streptococcus pneumoniae. Potential clinical implementation of the ct-DNA-based sensor was manifested in detecting the PYO in P. aeruginosa cultured baby diaper and sanitary napkin. Our results highlight that the newly developed ct-DNA-based sensing platform can be used as a potential candidate for real-time POC diagnosis of Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection in clinical samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Infections caused by multidrug-resistant bacteria remain a tremendous biomedical challenge to treat (Arias and Murray, 2009; Otto, 2012; Polly et al., 2022, van Duin and Paterson, 2016). The Infectious Diseases Society of America (IDSA) has recently released a report on disseminating effective clinical practices for combating the infection effectively (https://www.idsociety.org/practice-guideline/amrguidance/). The society also emphasized that the infection is caused by a group of microorganisms acronymically referred to as the “ESKAPE” pathogens (de Oliveira et al., 2020).

Among the ESKAPE pathogens, Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) is known for causing chronic lung infections, catheter-associated urinary tract infections (UTI), and delayed wound healing (Gomila et al., 2018). The virulence of P. aeruginosa, the severity of the infections and consequently, the antibiotic resistance is dependent on the bacterial cell population mediated by the quorum sensing network. Despite the prevailing challenges of P. aeruginosa infection, the standard diagnostic tests such as bacterial culture, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) practiced in clinical laboratories are cumbersome and cannot provide immediate results (Buchan and Ledeboer, 2014; Lazcka et al., 2007). For instance, the bacterial culture method requires culturing microorganisms on broth nutrient media with selective growth ingredients. This method would take days to weeks to get crucial information on pathogen identification. By that time, the subjects will be given multiple antibiotic doses, leading to increased antibiotic resistance of the P. aeruginosa biofilm, which makes the infections difficult to treat.

Despite the recent innovations in molecular detection protocols, rapid detection of P. aeruginosa is still challenging because of frequent modifications in protein secondary structure and binding domains as a result of antimicrobial resistance. It also makes the conventional antibody-based bioreceptors challenging to specifically recognize the target pathogens (Byrne et al., 2009; Hoyos-Nogués et al., 2018; Seok Kim et al., 2016). So, there is a need for rapid detection of P. aeruginosa infection at the initial stage of infection for efficient treatment. The presence of P. aeruginosa in clinical specimens can be identified and quantified through its quorum sensing (QS) molecules such as pyocyanin (PYO), phenazine-1- carboxamide (PCN), phenazine-1-carboxylic acid (PCA) and 5-methylphenazine-1-carboxylic acid (5-MCA) (Bellin et al., 2016, 2014). Among the QS molecules listed, PYO plays a critical role in biofilm formation (Saunders et al., 2020; Sharma et al., 2014; Wilt et al., 2019). Chemical sensors employing nanomaterials as recognition molecules and transducers have been attempted for electrochemical detection of PYO (Cernat et al., 2019; Elkhawaga et al., 2019; Rashid et al., 2021; Santiveri et al., 2018). Electroanalytical approaches have been developed as an alternate tool for the rapid detection of pathogenic bacteria for point-of-care (POC) measurement (Ahmed et al., 2014; Akhavan and Ghaderi, 2011; Capatina et al., 2022; Jain et al., 2021; Mitchell and Gu, 2004; Zhang et al., 2019). However, the selectivity and specificity of such electrochemical sensors are a big concern in complex biological matrixes due to the interfering agents, matrix effect, and the compromised affinity between the nanomaterial architecture and PYO. This issue could lead to false positive alarms, unreliable sensor readings, and insensitive measurements (Li et al., 2022).

There is a critical need for developing adaptive biosensing strategies using biorecognition elements, which could enable the sensitive and selective monitoring of target molecules in the clinical environment (Karuppaiah et al., 2022; Krishnan et al., 2022; Sapsford et al., 2006; Tabish et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2021; Zulfajri et al., 2020). In recent years, paper-based analytical devices and nanomaterial integrated probes, including carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have also emerged as POC sensing platform for disease identification (Ahmadivand et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2021b, 2021a; Lisa John et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2022) and potential candidate for therapeutic interventions (He et al., 2022; Jannesari et al., 2020; Rabiee et al., 2022). Very recently, it has been discovered that the electron transfer and interaction of PYO with extracellular double-stranded DNA is the key to QS and changing the physicochemical properties of P. aeruginosa (Das et al., 2015; Saunders et al., 2020). Although the hypothesis on PYO binding with double-stranded DNA has been documented, the experimental approach for validating the binding of PYO with DNA has not been demonstrated for diagnostics applications. In this investigation, for the first time, we are utilizing the biological interaction of PYO with DNA to develop a POC electrochemical sensor for detecting P. aeruginosa infection. The calf thymus (ct) - DNA conjugated on the surface of NH2-CQDs was used as a biorecognition element for the detection of PYO. Electrochemical approaches, including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and square wave voltammetry (SWV) are used to probe the interaction in real time. The biosensor was tested for measuring PYO levels in phosphate buffer saline (PBS), spiked artificial urine (AU) samples, P. aeruginosa growth culture medium (LB), and spiked real urine samples (RUS). For potential clinical applications, P. aeruginosa was grown on a baby diaper and sanitary napkin for mimicking the UTI, and the release of PYO from these specimens are detected using the electrochemical sensor.

P. aeruginosa strain PAO1 - MTCC 3541 was selected and purchased from Microbial Type Culture Collection and Gene Bank, Chandigarh. P. aeruginosa PAO1 was routinely grown in Luria Bertani (LB) medium (Difco Lennox, United States) and incubated in a controlled shaker (120 rpm) at room temperature for 12 h. The other clinical gram-positive and gram-negative pathogens (clinical isolates) were obtained as a gift from the Bioenergy & Bioremidiation lab, Alagappa University, Karaikudi. These strains were routinely grown in LB medium or Muller Hinton (MH) (Himedia -India) medium. All the strains were stored at −80 °C in a nutrient-rich media mixed with glycerol (40% (v/v)).

PYO was procured from Cayman Chemical, USA and the ct-DNA was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Potassium hexacyanoferrate (II), potassium hexacyanoferrate (III), potassium chloride (KCl), Tris TEA buffer and phosphate buffered saline were purchased from Himedia Laboratories. The chemicals required for preparing artificial urine media, such as CaCl2·2H2O, NaCl, Na2SO4, KH2PO4, KCl, NH4Cl, urea and creatinine were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry (TCI). Uric acid, ascorbic acid, NADPH, acetylsalicylic acid and glucose were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ammonia solutions were purchased from Analytical Reagent – RANKEM. All aqueous reagents and buffer solutions were freshly prepared with deionized (DI) water.

The NH2 functionalized CQDs (NH2-CQDs) were prepared using microwave-assisted pyrolysis of glucose. A solution of glucose (4 g in 3 mL DI water) was heated for 3 min in a household MW oven (700 W) (Tang et al., 2017). The color of the solution is turned from transparent to yellow and then to brown caramel-like fluid. The resultant solution is cooled, mixed with 50 mL DI water, and left undisturbed for 12 h. The resultant solution is then centrifuged for 15 min at 12,000 rpm to clear the contaminants. The residues collected are dried and lyophilized to get CQDs powder. Freshly prepared CQDs were mixed with a few drops of concentrated ammonia solution in a glass vial with a sealed cap to create NH2 functional groups on CQDs. The mixture was treated in an ultrasonic bath at 80 °C for 3 h. This mixture was centrifuged for 15 min at 4000 rpm to eliminate any excess ammonia, and the purified samples were kept oven-dried at 80 °C for 1 h (Wu et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface of the GCE (3 mm) was activated with slurries of alumina powder (Al2O3) with dimensions ranging from 1, 0.3 and 0.05 mm. The electrodes were then cleaned with DI water and dried at room temperature. The ct-DNA grafting solution was prepared by mixing an equal volume of Tris TEA buffer (5 mg/mL), and NH2-CQDs prepared in DI water (5 mg/mL). The solution is mixed thoroughly and placed in a refrigerator (4 °C) for 1 h. The ct-DNA are negatively charged molecules and has the affinity to bind with positively charged amine groups on NH2-CQDs through electrostatic interaction. The mixture (10 μL) was then deposited onto the GCE surface using a drop-casting approach. Following this, the electrode is washed three times with water and stored again in the refrigerator. The GCE electrode after this step of modification is denoted as ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs modified electrode.

The analytical applicability of the ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs modified electrode towards selective recognition of PYO was evaluated in laboratory standard solutions (1–100 μM) prepared using PBS, LB broth media containing PYO (1–100 μM), artificial urine medium (Parra et al., 2016) spiked with various concentrations of PYO (1–400 μM) and real urine sample spiked with PYO (1–250 μM). Release of PYO from P. aeruginosa cultured baby diaper and sanitary napkins were also assessed using the electrochemical PYO biosensor (Scheme 1 ). Square wave voltammetric (SWV) measurements were used to interrogate the ct-DNA conjugated sensor and to create a calibration curve. A three-electrode sensor configuration in which ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs modified GCE was used as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl and platinum wire (0.5 mm) was used as a reference and counter electrodes, respectively. An electrochemical cell containing 10 mL of the testing medium (PBS or LB broth or artificial urine or urine) is used as electrolyte for the detection of PYO. An electrochemical potential window of −0.5 to 0 V vs Ag/AgCl was used to probe the electrochemical response of PYO. To validate the selectivity, the electrochemical responses of the newly developed DNA biosensor towards PYO were recorded in the presence of other interfering analytes such as glucose, acetylsalicylic acid, NADPH, ascorbic acid and uric acid as potential interferences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of PYO release from P. aeruginosa PAO1
ce:section.ce:para: A single colony of P. aeruginosa strains was inoculated in a sterile LB broth and set in a shaking chamber (120 rpm) for incubation at room temperature overnight. A portion of the culture broth (1 mL) was collected and mixed with a freshly prepared sterile LB broth (9 mL) and incubated in the shaking chamber overnight. The bacterial suspensions were collected at different incubation intervals, such as 2, 12 and 24 h and centrifuged for 15 min (10,000 rpm) at 4 °C. The supernatant solutions were analyzed for PYO using ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs modified electrodes. The release of PYO from the pathogenic bacterial culture was also estimated using spectrophotometry, and the absorption spectra recorded at 375 nm were used for quantification. Similarly, single colonies of each bacterial strain (clinical isolate) such as Bacillus subtilis, Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, P. aeruginosa, Shigella dysenteriae, Staphylococcus aureus, and Streptococcus pneumoniae were also cultivated in 100 mL of sterilized LB broth and investigated for cross-reactivity analysis. The colony forming units (CFU) value at different time intervals was monitored for quantification of bacterial growth. The density of the bacteria was also measured at 600 nm optical densities (OD) at different time intervals to confirm the increasing bacterial count.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PYO in vitro in baby diaper and sanitary napkin surface and validation
ce:section.ce:para: P. aeruginosa PAO1 (1 × 107) was grown on baby diaper and sanitary napkin (both were cut into 1 × 1 cm pieces) in LB medium for 24 h. After bacterial culture growth, the baby diapter and sanitary were sonicated with 1 mL of PBS and artificial urine medium to collect the surface-adhered bacteria. The resultant solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 5 min at 4 °C. The supernatant solution containing PYO was analyzed using SWV. To confirm the release of PYO, the supernatant is purified in a 1:2 ratio of chloroform and analyzed using HPLC and NMR (Supplementary information).

The absorption spectrum of NH2-CQDs exhibited a sharp absorption peak with absorption peaks at 226 and 275 nm (Fig. 1 A) due to the 'π⁓π*' (aromatic –CC- bonds) and ‘n⁓π*' electron transition (-CO and -C-N) bonds respectively (Tang et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2019a). This sharp absorption peak at 226 nm indicates that the CQDs are homogeneity dispersed in solution. A red shift in the absorption maxima with quenching in the absorption signal was observed (248 nm) when ct-DNA was conjugated on the surface NH2-CQDs. The bathochromic shift indicates the successful conjugation of ct-DNA at the surface of NH2-CQDs. The fluorescence spectrum of the NH2-CQDs was investigated at various excitation wavelengths. The PL spectra showed an excitation-dependent emission indicating the amino group functionalization on the CQDs surface (Fig. 1B). Zeta potential measurements were used to analyse the surface charges on the ct-DNA, NH2-CQDs, and the stability of ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs conjugate (Fig. S1). ct-DNA is negatively charged biomolecule due to its constituent phosphate groups on the backbone structure. The zeta potential measurement of ct-DNA exhibits a negative zeta potential value of −33 mV, confirming the negatively charged surface. Amine functionalized CQDs shows a zeta potential value of +58 mV, indicating that the prepared NH2-CQDs in solution are stable and have positively charged surface. As expected, the zeta potential of ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs is shifted to −25 mV, indicating the stable conjugation of CQDs to the ct-DNA and the resultant conjugate has a net negative charge. The fluorescence spectra of NH2-CQDs recorded at various excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 1B. The excitation dependent emission behaviour clearly confirms the typical behaviour of CQDs. However, the fluorescence intensity of the NH2-CQDs was found to be bathochromic shift due to the ct-DNA conjugation on the surface of amine-functionalized CQDs (Fig. S2).

The FT-IR spectra recorded for CQDs and NH2-CQDs before and after the conjugation of ct-DNA are compared in Fig. 1C. The CQDs showed a broad peak at 3300 cm−1 and a small peak around 2931 cm−1 corresponding to –OH stretching vibrations. The CQDs also shows the CO stretching vibration and C–O–C vibrations at 1721 cm−1 and 780 cm−1 denoting the presence of the carbonyl group and ketones, respectively. The peak at fingerprint region (1625 cm−1) indicates the presence of –N-H bending in NH2-CQDs and the broad spectrum at 3480 cm−1 confirms N–H bending. For ct-DNA conjugated at the surface of NH2-CQDs, stretching vibrations at 3764 cm−1 and 3300 cm−1 are ascribed to –OH group present in ct-DNA. The peaks indicating the presence of alkyl amine group (1080 cm−1), C–O (1524 cm−1), and CO stretching (1702 cm−1 and 790 cm−1) further confirm the successful coupling of ct-DNA with NH2-CQDs. Raman spectroscopy is used to probe the structural and vibrational modes of the CQDs. Raman spectra of NH2-CQDs exhibited maximum intensity at 1360 and 1611 cm−1, corresponding to D and G bands of CQDs, respectively (Fig. 1D). The intensity ratio (I D/I G) was calculated to be 0.23 for NH2-CQDs. The low I D/I G ratio indicates that the NH2-CQDs prepared using the microwave-assisted method exhibit lower defects and heteroatomic groups on graphitic carbon network provides better electron-hole pair region suitable for electronic coupling to the successful functionalization of amine on the surface of CQDs. The peak at 1427 cm−1 is attributed to C–O–H due to the hydrogen bond between phosphate ion of ct-DNA molecule and amine group of CQDs. The characteristic peak at 1574 cm−1 belongs to the CO ring stretching vibration of cytosine of ct-DNA. The peaks at 1644 cm−1 and 1687 cm−1 are attributed to C–O and CO respectively due to the deformation of the hydrogen bond between CO of cytosine or formation of hydrocarbon along with Adenine (A) or Thymine (T) or Guanine (G). The characteristic peaks of disordered vibrating bands indicate the presence of (A), (C), (T), (G), deoxyribose and phosphate group which merged with NH2-CQDs.

X-ray diffraction pattern of NH2-CQDs provided a broad diffraction peak centered at 19.1° assigned to (002) peak disordered amorphous nature with graphitic structure (Fig. S3). The chemical composition of ct-DNA, NH2-CQDs, and the binding state of ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs was further investigated using XPS measurements. The XPS spectra of ct-DNA exhibited peaks in the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s regions (Fig. S4A). The deconvoluted carbon (C 1s) spectra of ct-DNA confirm the presence of functional groups such as C–C, C–H (284.4 eV), C–N, C–O (285.8 eV), C–OH, and C–O–C (286.8 eV) (Fig. S4B). The high resolution of nitrogen (N 1s) spectra showed predominant peaks located at 397.7 and 399.7 eV. The peaks at higher binding energy regions (399.7 eV) are attributed to the presence of the amino group (-NH2) and the peaks at the lower binding region (397.7 eV) confirm the existence of imino species (-NC) (Fig. 2 A). The deconvoluted O 1s spectra indicate the presence of functional groups such as CO and PO (531.1 eV), C–O–C, C–O–H, and C–O–P (532.2 eV) in ct-DNA (Fig. 2B). The high-resolution spectra of phosphorous (P 2p) exhibited a strong peak centered at 133.1 eV which can be split into two peaks (doublet) corresponding to 2p1/2 (132.7 eV) and 2p3/2 (133.6 eV) spin-orbits (Fig. 2C). The XPS survey spectrum of NH2-CQDs (Fig. S5 A-D) exhibited three major peaks at 282.9, 396.8, and 529.5 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s core levels, respectively. The deconvoluted C 1s spectrum showed major peaks at 282.1 (CC), 283.0 (C–N), 283.7 (C–O), and 285.2 eV (CO/CN). The deconvoluted N 1s spectrum NH2-CQDs of displayed three typical peaks at 396.9, 397.4, and 398.7 eV corresponding to the pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and graphitic N, respectively. Two main peaks were observed corresponding to the functional groups such as C–OH (528.5 eV) and CO (529.4 eV) in the O 1s spectra of NH2-CQDs. The XPS survey spectrum of ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs (Fig. S5 C-D) exhibited three major peaks at 286, 399.9, 533.3, and 134.7 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and P 2p core levels, respectively. The XPS spectra recorded for ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs are shown in (Fig. 2D–F). The binding energy observed for P in ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs is increased from 133.1 to 133.7 eV. The corresponding energy levels assigned to 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 spin-orbit peaks are also increased subsequently, demonstrating that the backbone phosphate molecules in ct-DNA play an active role in electrostatic interactions. Similarly, the binding energies observed for the N 1s chemical species such as –NH2 and –NC are increased to 399.3 and 400.9 eV, respectively. The changes in binding energy values corresponding to CO/PO and C–O–C/C–O–H/C–O–P are also confirming the participation of phosphate and carbonyl groups in the electrostatic interaction. The peak observed at 535.4 eV is ascribed to the binding energy of oxygen associated with the water molecule entrapped in the film. Overall, the XPS results confirm the electrostatic interactions between positively charged NH2-CQDs and negatively charged phosphate group (PO4 −) in the DNA backbone. The proposed mechanism for conjugation of ct-DNA with and NH2-CQDs through electrostatic interaction (Fig. 2G). The interlayer spacing was calculated to be 0.34 nm corresponding to the graphite carbon structure with space group P 63/MMC. HR-TEM images indicate that the synthesized of NH2-CQDs exhibit a spherical shape and are monodispersed (Fig. S5E). In the selected area, electron diffraction (SAED) illustrated in insert Fig. S5E confirms their graphitic carbon characteristics. The distribution of particle dimensions ranges from 2 to 6 nm with a mean size of 4 nm with a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm (Fig. S5F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs sensor assembly
ce:section.ce:para: The step-by-step characterization of the ct-DNA-based PYO sensor assembly was characterized using CV and EIS techniques. Measurements were performed in an external redox probe, [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- as both the CQDs and ct-DNA do not produce any significant faradaic current responses (Fig. 3 A). Electrochemical responses observed for bare GCE are used as control. The CV recorded for the plain electrode showed an excellent peak potential separation (ΔE p) of 98 mV and an oxidation peak current value (I pa) of ∼40 μA corresponding to the external redox probe. Deposition of NH2-CQDs leads to a decrease in I pa to ∼10 μA, while shifting the ΔE p to 138 mV, indicating the semiconducting nature of CQDs, which blocks the electron transfer of the redox probe. Conjugation of ct-DNA further decreased I pa to ∼5 μA while increasing the ΔE p to 158 mV. The results demonstrate that the ct-DNA is conjugated on the surface of NH2-CQDs modified electrode, which further hinders the electron transfer features of the redox probe. Faradaic EIS measurements were executed to corroborate the results of the CV technique. In a typical faradaic EIS technique, the impedance results are described as a component of real (Z’) and imaginary (Z”) impedances (Fig. 3B). The impedance data can be interpreted using a Randles equivalent circuit, which includes a bulk solution resistance (Rs) in series to the parallel combination of charge-transfer resistance (Rct), double-layer capacitance (Cdl) and Warburg impedance (Zw). The variations in the diameter of the semicircle region in the Nyquist plot (Rct) can be used to correlate the energy barrier of the external redox mediator to reach the electrode. The Rct value of the bare electrode is increased from 19 Ω to 222 Ω after the deposition of NH2-CQDs. The charge transfer resistance further increased to 407 Ω for the ct-DNA modified electrode. The results confirm that the modification of the electrode surface with each layer of CQDs and ct-DNA resulted in an increase in the steric hindrance of the external redox mediator to reach the surface of the electrode. The finding also corroborates the results from CV techniques confirming the successful conjugation of ct-DNA on the surface of the NH2-CQDs modified electrode.

The real electroactive area (A real ) of the ct-DNA modified electrode can also be determined using CV measurements. Randles–Ševćik equation (Eq (1)) (García-Miranda Ferrari et al., 2018) for a reversible system is used for calculating A real .
(1)
I
p , f
r e v
= ± 0.446
n F
A
r e a l
C
n F D ν
R T
where
I
p , f
r e v
is the forward peak current of the reversible redox system, n is the number of electrons involved in the redox reaction, F is the Faraday constant (C mol−1),
A
r e a l
is active electrode area, C and D are molar-concentration and diffusion coefficient of the redox probe (cm2 s−1), T is temperature (in Kelvin) R is the universal gas constant. For calculating the A real from CV response and Randles–Ševćik equation, the ct-DNA modified sensor is recorded at various scan rates (10–100 mV s−1). A plot of forward peak current vs applied CV scan rates was constructed (Fig. 3C). The gradient calculated from the plot of Log Ip vs. Log ν was found to be close to 1, indicating a thin-layer diffusion effect of the ct-DNA modified electrode. The active electrode area calculated for the ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs and NH2-CQDs is found to be 3.1313 cm2 and 9.31679 cm2, respectively (Fig. 3D).

A broad reversible peak with cathodic and anodic peak potentials at −0.251 and −0.196 V vs Ag/AgCl with a ΔE p of −0.25 V corresponding to reversible redox transformation of PYO (Elliott et al., 2017) was detected at the ct-DNA modified electrode (Fig. S6). However, the bare GCE did not show any reversible redox peak for PYO, demonstrating a sluggish electrochemical behavior of the GCE in the presence of PYO. The electrochemical measurement of pathogen detection is influenced by the test solution pH and bacterial growth conditions. Thus, the effect of pH on the newly developed ct-DNA-based biosensor performance for monitoring PYO is investigated (Fig. S7a) for a wide pH range (pH 3–10). The peak potential of PYO shifts to a more negative value as the pH increased from 3 to 10, implying that protons are involved in the electrochemical reaction. Linear behaviour of shift in potential is observed across the selected pH range. The slope value calculated to be ΔE/ΔpH = −65.2 ± 0.06 mV/pH (Fig. S7b), which is closely related to the theoretical Nernstian behavior of −59 mV/pH unit for the equal number of hydrogen ions and electrons involved in an electrochemical reaction (Do et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performances of ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: In a clinical scenario, PYO levels vary between 1.3 and 31 μg/mL in urine and cultured samples of infected persons (Elbargisy, 2021). Since the sensor calibration needs to be performed in a virtual or similar environment to that of the real-world sample, calibration curves were prepared in three different electrolyte mediums. The phosphate solution (1–100 μM) and artificial urine (1–400 μM) were selected as the testing mediums. The redox peak corresponding to PYO is increased with an increase in concentration in all three mediums tested. In PBS, the redox potential observed for PYO oxidation is found to be −0.23 V (vs Ag/AgCl) (Fig. 4 A), while for the artificial urine medium, the peak potential is −0.15 V (Fig. 4B). This finding could be attributed to the medium compositions, which could affect the diffusion of redox reporter and, as a result, the signal strength of ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs modified electrode. Although the peak potential varies with the medium, the developed PYO sensor is supported by an excellent linear relationship with linearity between the concentration of PYO and the oxidation peak current (Fig. 4C–D). This finding implies that the conjugated ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs modified electrode is suitable for detecting PYO in different solutions with a less matrix effect. Table S1 shows the comparison of the analytical parameter of the newly developed sensor to the previous studies conducted in the electrochemical sensing of PYO. The limit of detection (LOD) value is calculated using the formula, LOD = 3σ/S, where σ = standard deviation of SWV blank solution and S = slope value from the calibration curve. The calculated LOD values are 69 nM for PBS, 81 nM for artificial urine medium, 93 nM for culture growth medium (LB), and 37 nM for the real urine sample. The sensor showed a good linear range and detection limit compared to the other reported PYO sensing platform (Table S1).

The influence of commonly found clinically relevant interfering electroactive species such as glucose, acetylsalicylic acid, NADPH, ascorbic acid, and uric acid on the sensor response is investigated at a 10-fold increase in concentration. The sensor selectively responds to PYO (10 μM) in the presence of 10-fold excess concentrations (100 μM) of the interfering analytes. The results indicate that determining PYO at ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs modified electrode is highly feasible even at higher concentrations of interfering molecules. The time constant of the affinity-based biosensor is crucial for selecting an appropriate interrogation technique and time resolution required for the analysis. DNA-based electrochemical sensors association/dissociation constants rate ranges in tens of milliseconds (Pellitero et al., 2020) and allows the continuous long-term monitoring of target molecules. The electrochemical response of the ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs modified electrode was examined at different incubation time resolutions (5–30 min) in the presence of PYO (10 μM) in PBS at pH 7 (Fig. S8a). In addition, the repeatability of the sensor platform was also examined by adding PYO (each addition 10 μM) in an artificial urine medium at pH 7 (Fig. S9 a-c). The selectivity (Fig. S8b) and reproducibility (Fig. S8c and Fig. S10 a-c) of the ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs biosensing platform were also tested in the presence of 10 μM PYO spiked artificial urine samples. These results indicated that the developed PYO sensor showed excellent repeatability and reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical PYO detection in P. aeruginosa – PAO1 (MTCC 3541) culture
ce:section.ce:para: After a successful demonstration for monitoring PYO in artificial urine and bacterial culture, the newly developed sensor was attempted for direct detection of PYO produced by the culture of P. aeruginosa – PAO1 (MTCC 3541). It is a known fact that the growth of P. aeruginosa biofilm is mediated through PYO quorum sensing. In clinical testing, 24–48 h are required to confirm the presence of the P. aeruginosa pathogen. To validate the performance, the biosensor was used to detect the PYO secretion from P. aeruginosa during the 24 h period. SWV was used to probe the signal observed for PYO (a sharp redox peak at −0.22 V vs Ag/AgCl) release, which can be used as a direct measure of P. aeruginosa levels. The results indicated that PYO was not produced in the P. aeruginosa – PAO1 strain after 2 h of incubation (lag phase) and detected after 12 and 24 h the culture suspension after reaching the stationary phase (Fig. S11). For P. aeruginosa – PAO1 strain with PYO release was observed. Interestingly, no visible peak was recorded in the control sample (LB broth without P. aeruginosa - PAO1). The results observed using electrochemical measurements corroborate well with the spectrophotometric studies (Fig. S12). This evidence is similar to the findings that the production of PYO is controlled by a quorum detection system that does not exist in the early stages of culture as reported by (Cabeen, 2014; Elkhawaga et al., 2019). For LB medium and real urine sample (Mounesh Venugopal Reddy and Nagaraja, 2021; Mounesh and Mounes Reddy, 2020), the redox potential observed for PYO oxidation is found to be −0.23 V and −0.29 (vs Ag/AgCl) (Fig. 5 A–B), respectively. The developed PYO sensor is supported by an excellent linear relationship with a relative between the concentration of PYO and the oxidation peak current produced (Fig. 5C–E). Further, we performed undiluted real urine samples (healthy volunteers) with 10 μM PYO (Fig. S13) by SWV techniques. The calculated LOD values are 93 nM for the LB medium and 37 nM for the real urine sample. These results indicate that the developed PYO sensor can be used for early detection of patients who are infected with P. aeruginosa (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical interference of other bacterial cultures
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to P. aeruginosa, other pathogenic bacteria species also release quorum-sensing molecules which could potentially interfere with the electrochemical sensor response in the potential window used for PYO detection. Therefore, various types of clinical bacterial isolates such as Bacillus subtilis, Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Shigella dysenteriae, Staphylococcus aureus, and Streptococcus pneumoniae have been cultured and the extracts collected were analyzed using the ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs biosensor. The SWV studies show that PYO release was observed only in P. aeruginosa - PAO1 and clinically isolated P. aeruginosa strains (Fig. S14). Other clinical bacterial isolates did not show significant redox characteristics similar to PYO in the selected potential window. This finding proves that the electrochemical signal produced by the conjugated ct-DNA/NH2-CQDs modified electrode is specific to PYO secreted from P. aeruginosa species. The other redox-active QS molecules of P. aeruginosa species such as PCN, PCA, and 5-MCA did not showed any observable electrochemical signal, due to their slow redox reactions. These QS molecules are produced during the decline phase of bacterial growth and has higher redox potential which do not interfere with PYO electrochemical detection (Bellin et al., 2016, 2014; Oziat et al., 2021, 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical signal in vivo in diaper and napkin
ce:section.ce:para: P. aeruginosa is the leading cause of UTI in infants and women. To investigate the potential applicability for clinical measurement, P. aeruginosa –PAO1 were artificially cultured in baby diapers and sanitary napkin to simulate the conditions of UTI. The bacterial sample extracted from the napkin and diaper were tested for the presence of PYO. Interestingly, matrix supernatant collected in PBS and artificial urine medium analyzed using SWV showed the presence of PYO (Fig. S15 a-b). Morphological analysis using SEM confirms the growth of P. aeruginosa on the diaper and napkin surfaces (Fig. S15 c – d). The supernatants collected from the napkins and diapers were also analyzed using HPLC and NMR to confirm the secretion of PYO by P. aeruginosa –PAO1 (Figs. S16 and S17).

In conclusion, a unique biosensor for PYO was developed by conjugating ct-DNA on the surface of NH2-CQDs by mimicking PYO biological electron transfer function. The detection limit of the proposed sensor platform for detecting PYO in PBS, artificial urine, culture media, and real urine samples are 69 nM, 81 nM, 93 nM, and 37 nM, respectively. The sensor showed good selectivity, reliability, repeatability, and reproducibility for detecting PYO levels in biological samples. The application of the proposed biosensing strategy has been demonstrated in artificially simulated UTI conditions in baby diapers and sanitary napkin. The newly developed biosensor can detect the P. aeruginosa at the initial stage of infection and has the potential to be integrated into wearable artefacts for early detection. The findings will also aid in reducing the emergence of multidrug-resistant P. aeruginosa by providing early therapeutic interventions.
CECRI/PESVC/Pubs./2022–102.

Boobalan Thulasinathan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sujatha D: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Sethupathi Murugan: Formal analysis, Methodology. Subhendu K. Panda: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Murugan Veerapandian: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Pandiaraj Manickam: Supervision, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: BT and PM thank the Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), Government of India for the award of ICMR-ITR grant – Research Associate Fellowship (File No.: 5/3/8/68/ITR-F/2022-ITR, dated: February 21, 2022). SD acknowledges funding from UGC-JRF (NTA Ref. No. 201610108152 dated April 01, 2021). Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF), CSIR-Central Electrochemical Research Institute, Karaikudi acknowledged for characterization facilities.
